text: The surgeon demonstrates the intracorporeal placement of an anvil for a sigmoid resection in a morbidly obese patient. The anvil is inserted through the fan and steel incision, which will later be used for specimen extraction. The area of demarcation is visible where the surgeon's left hand is holding the bowel.

The surgeon prepares to divide the specimen at the proximal margin using a surgical stapler. Despite the inability to use ICG, visible color changes indicate adequate perfusion, and a clip is placed to the left of the stapler where pulsatile blood flow is observed.
The surgeon closes the colotomy using a VLOC suture to prevent spillage of bowel contents or tumor cells during specimen extraction.
The surgeon uses a stapler to perform a distal anastomosis, with visible color change indicating adequate blood flow in the tissue.

The surgeon places the EEA stapler trans-anally, carefully pulling the anterior staple line posteriorly before bringing the spike out. A leak test is performed, which is negative, indicating no leaks.
